Overview of BayesianQDM

Source: vignettes/overview.Rmd
overview.Rmd

Introduction

The BayesianQDM package provides a comprehensive framework for Bayesian Quantitative Decision-Making (QDM) in clinical trials. It implements a three-zone Go/NoGo/Gray decision rule under two complementary probability metrics:

  • Posterior probability: quantifies the evidence from observed PoC data that the treatment effect exceeds a clinically meaningful threshold.
  • Posterior predictive probability: quantifies the probability that a future (Phase III) trial would succeed given the observed PoC results.

Both metrics are available for single and two-endpoint analyses, covering binary and continuous outcomes, across three study designs (controlled, uncontrolled, external control). The package also provides grid-search functions (getgamma family) to find optimal Go/NoGo thresholds satisfying user-specified operating characteristic criteria.

1. Bayesian Model and Posterior Distributions

1.1 Binary Endpoint: Beta-Binomial Conjugate Model

Let \(\pi_j\) denote the response rate in group \(j\) (\(j = t\): treatment, \(j = c\): control). We place independent Beta priors on each rate:

\[\pi_j \sim \mathrm{Beta}(a_j,\, b_j), \quad j = t, c.\]

Given \(n_j\) patients with \(y_j\) responders, the posterior is

\[\pi_j \mid y_j \;\sim\; \mathrm{Beta}\!\left(a_j + y_j,\; b_j + n_j - y_j\right).\]

The treatment effect of interest is the difference \(\theta = \pi_t - \pi_c\). Because the two groups are independent, the posterior of \(\theta\) is the distribution of the difference of two independent Beta random variables, which has no closed-form CDF. The package evaluates

\[P(\theta > \theta_0 \mid y_t, y_c) = P(\pi_t - \pi_c > \theta_0 \mid y_t, y_c)\]

by numerical integration via pbetadiff():

\[P(\pi_t - \pi_c > \theta_0) = \int_{\max(0,\,\theta_0)}^{1} f_{\mathrm{Beta}(a_t^*, b_t^*)}(u)\; \bigl[1 - F_{\mathrm{Beta}(a_c^*, b_c^*)}(u - \theta_0)\bigr]\, du,\]

where \(a_j^* = a_j + y_j\) and \(b_j^* = b_j + n_j - y_j\) are the posterior shape parameters.

1.2 Continuous Endpoint: Normal-Inverse-Chi-Squared Conjugate Model

Let \(\mu_j\) and \(\sigma_j^2\) denote the mean and variance of the outcome in group \(j\) (\(j = t, c\)). Under the Normal-Inverse-Chi-squared (N-Inv-\(\chi^2\)) conjugate prior

\[(\mu_j,\, \sigma_j^2) \;\sim\; \mathrm{N\text{-}Inv\text{-}\chi^2} (\mu_{0j},\, \kappa_{0j},\, \nu_{0j},\, \sigma_{0j}^2),\]

where \(\mu_{0j}\) is the prior mean, \(\kappa_{0j}\) the prior precision (pseudo-sample size), \(\nu_{0j}\) the prior degrees of freedom, and \(\sigma_{0j}^2\) the prior scale (corresponding to mu0_t/mu0_c, kappa0_t/kappa0_c, nu0_t/nu0_c, sigma0_t/sigma0_c).

Given sample mean \(\bar{y}_j\) (bar_y_t, bar_y_c), sample standard deviation \(s_j\) (s_t, s_c), and sample size \(n_j\) (n_t, n_c), the posterior parameters are updated as

\[\kappa_{nj} = \kappa_{0j} + n_j, \qquad \nu_{nj} = \nu_{0j} + n_j,\]

\[\mu_{nj} = \frac{\kappa_{0j}\,\mu_{0j} + n_j\,\bar{y}_j}{\kappa_{nj}},\]

\[\sigma_{nj}^2 = \frac{\nu_{0j}\,\sigma_{0j}^2 + (n_j-1)\,s_j^2 + \dfrac{n_j\,\kappa_{0j}}{\kappa_{nj}}(\mu_{0j} - \bar{y}_j)^2}{\nu_{nj}}.\]

The marginal posterior of \(\mu_j\) is a non-standardised \(t\)-distribution:

\[\mu_j \mid \bar{y}_j, s_j \;\sim\; t_{\nu_{nj}}\!\left(\mu_{nj},\; \frac{\sigma_{nj}^2}{\kappa_{nj}}\right),\]

i.e., location \(\mu_{nj}\), scale \(\sigma_{nj}/\sqrt{\kappa_{nj}}\), and \(\nu_{nj}\) degrees of freedom.

Vague (Jeffreys) prior — the limiting case \(\kappa_{0j} \to 0\), \(\nu_{0j} \to 0\) yields

\[\mu_j \mid \bar{y}_j, s_j \;\sim\; t_{n_j - 1}\!\left(\bar{y}_j,\; \frac{s_j}{\sqrt{n_j}}\right).\]

The posterior of the treatment effect \(\theta = \mu_t - \mu_c\) is the distribution of the difference of two independent non-standardised \(t\)-variables. Three methods are provided to evaluate \(P(\theta > \theta_0 \mid \bar{y}_t, s_t, \bar{y}_c, s_c)\); see Section 3 for details.

2. Posterior Predictive Probability

2.1 Binary Endpoint

Let \(\tilde{y}_j \mid \pi_j \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(m_j, \pi_j)\) denote future trial outcomes in group \(j\) (\(j = t, c\)) with future sample size \(m_j\) (m_t, m_c). Integrating out the unknown response rates under their posteriors gives the posterior predictive (Beta-Binomial) distribution:

\[P(\tilde{y}_j = k) = \binom{m_j}{k} \frac{B(a_j^* + k,\; b_j^* + m_j - k)}{B(a_j^*,\, b_j^*)}, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, m_j,\]

where \(B(\cdot,\cdot)\) is the Beta function. The posterior predictive probability of a successful future trial is then

\[P\!\left(\tilde{\theta} > \theta_0 \mid y_t, y_c\right) = \sum_{k_t=0}^{m_t}\sum_{k_c=0}^{m_c} \mathbf{1}[k_t/m_t - k_c/m_c > \theta_0]\; P(\tilde{y}_t = k_t)\; P(\tilde{y}_c = k_c),\]

evaluated exactly by pbetabinomdiff().

2.2 Continuous Endpoint

The predictive distribution of a single future observation \(\tilde{y}_j\) under the N-Inv-\(\chi^2\) posterior is a non-standardised \(t\)-distribution. For the future sample mean \(\bar{\tilde{y}}_j\) based on \(m_j\) future patients, the scale is reduced by \(\sqrt{m_j}\):

\[\bar{\tilde{y}}_j \mid \bar{y}_j, s_j \;\sim\; t_{\nu_{nj}}\!\left(\mu_{nj},\; \sigma_{nj}\sqrt{\frac{1 + \kappa_{nj}}{\kappa_{nj}\, m_j}}\right).\]

Under the vague prior (\(\kappa_{0j} = \nu_{0j} = 0\), so \(\kappa_{nj} = n_j\), \(\sigma_{nj} = s_j\)) this becomes

\[\bar{\tilde{y}}_j \mid \bar{y}_j, s_j \;\sim\; t_{n_j - 1}\!\left(\bar{y}_j,\; s_j\sqrt{\frac{n_j + 1}{n_j\, m_j}}\right).\]

The posterior predictive probability of the future treatment effect exceeding \(\theta_0\) is

\[P\!\left(\bar{\tilde{y}}_t - \bar{\tilde{y}}_c > \theta_0 \mid \bar{y}_t, s_t, \bar{y}_c, s_c\right),\]

computed by the same three methods as the posterior probability (Section 3), with the predictive scale replacing the posterior scale.

3. Computation Methods for Continuous Endpoints

The core computational challenge is evaluating the CDF of the difference \(D = T_t - T_c\) of two independent non-standardised \(t\)-variables

\[T_j \sim t_{\nu_j}(\mu_j, \sigma_j), \qquad j = t, c,\]

at a threshold \(\theta_0\). Three methods are available.

3.1 Numerical Integration (NI)

The CDF is expressed as a one-dimensional integral by integrating out \(T_t\):

\[P(D \le \theta_0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F_{t_{\nu_c}(\mu_c, \sigma_c)}\!\left(t - \theta_0\right)\; f_{t_{\nu_t}(\mu_t, \sigma_t)}(t)\; dt,\]

where \(F_{t_\nu(\mu,\sigma)}\) denotes the CDF and \(f_{t_\nu(\mu,\sigma)}\) the PDF of the non-standardised \(t\)-distribution. This integral is evaluated by stats::integrate() (adaptive Gauss-Kronrod quadrature) in ptdiff_NI(). This method is exact but evaluates the integral once per call; it is therefore suitable for single-probability evaluations but slow for large-scale simulations.

3.2 Monte Carlo Simulation (MC)

Independent samples are drawn from each marginal distribution:

\[T_t^{(i)} \sim t_{\nu_t}(\mu_t, \sigma_t), \quad T_c^{(i)} \sim t_{\nu_c}(\mu_c, \sigma_c), \quad i = 1, \ldots, n_{\mathrm{MC}},\]

and the probability is estimated as the empirical proportion:

\[\widehat{P}(D > \theta_0) = \frac{1}{n_{\mathrm{MC}}} \sum_{i=1}^{n_{\mathrm{MC}}} \mathbf{1}\!\left[T_t^{(i)} - T_c^{(i)} > \theta_0\right].\]

The package implements this in ptdiff_MC() using a vectorised approach: when called from pbayesdecisionprob1cont() with a vector of \(n_{\mathrm{sim}}\) scenarios, all draws are generated as a single \(n_{\mathrm{MC}} \times n_{\mathrm{sim}}\) matrix to avoid repeated loop overhead.

3.3 Moment-Matching Approximation (MM)

\(D = T_t - T_c\) is approximated by a single non-standardised \(t\)-distribution \(t_{\nu^*}(\mu^*, \sigma^*)\) by matching the first and second even moments. For \(T_j \sim t_{\nu_j}(\mu_j, \sigma_j)\) with \(\nu_j > 4\):

\[E[T_j] = \mu_j, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(T_j) = \sigma_j^2\,\frac{\nu_j}{\nu_j - 2}, \qquad E[(T_j - \mu_j)^4] = \frac{3\nu_j^2\,\sigma_j^4}{(\nu_j-2)(\nu_j-4)}.\]

The mean of \(D\) is matched directly:

\[\mu^* = \mu_t - \mu_c.\]

Since \(T_t\) and \(T_c\) are independent, the second and fourth central moments of \(D\) are

\[\mathrm{Var}(D) = \sigma_t^2\,\frac{\nu_t}{\nu_t - 2} + \sigma_c^2\,\frac{\nu_c}{\nu_c - 2},\]

\[E[(D - \mu^*)^4] = \frac{3\nu_t^2\,\sigma_t^4}{(\nu_t-2)(\nu_t-4)} + \frac{3\nu_c^2\,\sigma_c^4}{(\nu_c-2)(\nu_c-4)} + 6\,\mathrm{Var}(T_t)\,\mathrm{Var}(T_c).\]

Denoting \(Q^* = \{\mathrm{Var}(D)\}^2\) and \(Q = E[(D-\mu^*)^4]/3\), the effective degrees of freedom are obtained by equating the normalised fourth moment of \(D\) to that of \(t_{\nu^*}\):

\[\nu^* = \frac{2Q^* - 4Q}{Q^* - Q}.\]

The scale parameter follows from the second-moment equation:

\[\sigma^* = \sqrt{\mathrm{Var}(D)\,\frac{\nu^* - 2}{\nu^*}}.\]

This yields a fully closed-form, vectorised approximation implemented in ptdiff_MM(), making it the recommended method for large-scale simulation studies. The approximation requires \(\nu_t > 4\) and \(\nu_c > 4\).

4. Study Designs

4.1 Controlled Design

The standard parallel-group randomised controlled trial (RCT) with concurrent treatment (\(j = t\)) and control (\(j = c\)) groups. Both posterior distributions are updated from the observed PoC data, and the probability is evaluated for the treatment effect \(\theta = \pi_t - \pi_c\) (binary) or \(\theta = \mu_t - \mu_c\) (continuous).

4.2 Uncontrolled Design (Single-Arm with Hypothetical Control)

When no concurrent control group is available (single-group PoC trial), the control distribution is specified from external knowledge rather than observed data.

Binary endpoint: a hypothetical responder count \(z\) is specified for the control group of size \(n_c\), yielding posterior shape parameters \(a_c^* = a_c + z\) and \(b_c^* = b_c + n_c - z\).

Continuous endpoint: the hypothetical control mean \(\mu_{0c}\) (mu0_c) is fixed from prior knowledge and the control variance is scaled relative to the treatment posterior variance via a scaling factor \(r > 0\):

\[\sigma_c = \sqrt{r} \cdot \sigma_t,\]

so the scale of the control \(t\)-distribution is \(\sqrt{r}\) times that of the treatment.

4.3 External Design (Power Prior)

Historical or external data are incorporated through a power prior with borrowing weight \(\alpha_{0e} \in (0, 1]\). Under the Beta-Binomial model for group \(j\), the power prior augments the initial prior by a fraction \(\alpha_{0ej}\) (alpha0e_t, alpha0e_c) of the external data sufficient statistics:

\[(\alpha_j^*,\; \beta_j^*) = \left(a_j + \alpha_{0ej}\, y_{ej},\;\; b_j + \alpha_{0ej}(n_{ej} - y_{ej})\right),\]

where \(n_{ej}\) (ne_t, ne_c) and \(y_{ej}\) (ye_t, ye_c) are the external sample size and responder count for group \(j\). These augmented parameters then serve as the prior for the current PoC data, yielding a closed-form posterior without MCMC.

Under the N-Inv-\(\chi^2\) model, the power prior inflates the prior precision and degrees of freedom by the weighted external sample:

\[\kappa_{0j}^* = \alpha_{0ej}\, n_{ej}, \quad \nu_{0j}^* = \alpha_{0ej}\, n_{ej} - 1,\]

\[\mu_{0j}^* = \bar{y}_{ej}, \quad \sigma_{0j}^{*2} = s_{ej}^2,\]

leading to the posterior updates

\[\mu_{nj} = \frac{\alpha_{0ej} n_{ej}\, \bar{y}_{ej} + n_j\, \bar{y}_j} {\alpha_{0ej} n_{ej} + n_j},\]

\[\sigma_{nj}^2 = \frac{\alpha_{0ej}(n_{ej}-1) s_{ej}^2 + (n_j-1) s_j^2 + \dfrac{\alpha_{0ej} n_{ej} n_j (\bar{y}_{ej} - \bar{y}_j)^2} {\alpha_{0ej} n_{ej} + n_j}} {\alpha_{0ej} n_{ej} + n_j},\]

with degrees of freedom \(\nu_{nj} = \alpha_{0ej} n_{ej} + n_j - 1\). Setting \(\alpha_{0ej} = 1\) corresponds to full borrowing; \(\alpha_{0ej} \to 0\) recovers the vague-prior result from the current data alone.

5. The Three-Zone Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Framework

5.1 Single Endpoint

The Go/NoGo/Gray decision rule uses two pre-specified thresholds \(\gamma_{\mathrm{go}} > \gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}}\) (gamma_go, gamma_nogo) applied to a pair of probabilities \((g_{\mathrm{go}},\, g_{\mathrm{nogo}})\):

\[\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Go} & \text{if } g_{\mathrm{go}} \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{go}} \text{ AND } g_{\mathrm{nogo}} < \gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}} \\ \text{NoGo} & \text{if } g_{\mathrm{go}} < \gamma_{\mathrm{go}} \text{ AND } g_{\mathrm{nogo}} \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}} \\ \text{Miss} & \text{if } g_{\mathrm{go}} \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{go}} \text{ AND } g_{\mathrm{nogo}} \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}} \\ \text{Gray} & \text{otherwise (neither Go nor NoGo criterion met).} \end{cases}\]

A Miss occurs when both Go and NoGo criteria are simultaneously satisfied, which indicates an inconsistent threshold configuration. By default the functions stop with an error if Miss probability \(> 0\) (error_if_Miss = TRUE).

For posterior probability, \(\theta_{\mathrm{TV}}\) (theta_TV, Target Value) and \(\theta_{\mathrm{MAV}}\) (theta_MAV, Minimum Acceptable Value, \(\theta_{\mathrm{TV}} > \theta_{\mathrm{MAV}}\)) define two separate probabilities:

\[g_{\mathrm{go}} = P(\theta > \theta_{\mathrm{TV}} \mid y_t, y_c), \qquad g_{\mathrm{nogo}} = P(\theta \le \theta_{\mathrm{MAV}} \mid y_t, y_c).\]

For posterior predictive probability, a single null threshold \(\theta_{\mathrm{NULL}}\) (theta_NULL) is used and the same probability serves both roles:

\[g_{\mathrm{go}} = g_{\mathrm{nogo}} = P(\tilde{\theta} > \theta_{\mathrm{NULL}} \mid y_t, y_c),\]

so the decision reduces to Go if \(g \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{go}}\), NoGo if \(g < \gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}}\), and Gray otherwise (Miss cannot occur in this case).

5.2 Operating Characteristics

For a given true parameter value (e.g., true treatment effect), the operating characteristics are the marginal probabilities of each decision outcome:

\[\Pr(\mathrm{Go}) = \sum_{y_t, y_c} \mathbf{1}[\mathrm{decision}(y_t, y_c) = \mathrm{Go}]\; P(y_t, y_c \mid \text{true parameter}),\]

and analogously for \(\Pr(\mathrm{NoGo})\) and \(\Pr(\mathrm{Gray})\), where \((y_t, y_c)\) denotes the observed data and the sum (or integral for continuous outcomes) is taken over all possible outcomes under the true parameter.

For binary endpoints, the exact marginal probabilities over all possible outcome pairs \((y_t, y_c) \in \{0,\ldots,n_t\} \times \{0,\ldots,n_c\}\) are computed by pbayesdecisionprob1bin() using binomial probability weights:

\[\Pr(\mathrm{Go}) = \sum_{y_t=0}^{n_t} \sum_{y_c=0}^{n_c} \mathbf{1}\!\left[g_{\mathrm{go}}(y_t, y_c) \ge \gamma_{\mathrm{go}} \;\text{ and }\; g_{\mathrm{nogo}}(y_t, y_c) < \gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}}\right]\; \binom{n_t}{y_t} \pi_t^{y_t}(1-\pi_t)^{n_t-y_t}\; \binom{n_c}{y_c} \pi_c^{y_c}(1-\pi_c)^{n_c-y_c}.\]

For continuous endpoints, the operating characteristics are estimated by Monte Carlo simulation over \(n_{\mathrm{sim}}\) replicates in pbayesdecisionprob1cont(): for each replicate \(i\), a dataset \((\bar{y}_t^{(i)}, s_t^{(i)}, \bar{y}_c^{(i)}, s_c^{(i)})\) is generated from the true parameter distribution, the probability \(g^{(i)}\) is computed, and the decision is recorded. Empirical proportions over all replicates yield the estimated operating characteristics.

5.3 Optimal Threshold Search

The getgamma family of functions finds the optimal pair \((\gamma_{\mathrm{go}}, \gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}})\) by grid search over a user-supplied candidate grid \(\Gamma = \{\gamma^{(1)}, \ldots, \gamma^{(K)}\} \subset (0, 1)\). A two-stage precompute-then-sweep strategy is used for efficiency:

Stage 1 (Precomputation): all posterior/predictive probabilities \(g(y_t, y_c)\) (binary) or \(g^{(i)}\) (continuous, over \(n_{\mathrm{sim}}\) replicates) are computed once, independently of \(\gamma\).

Stage 2 (Gamma sweep): for each candidate \(\gamma \in \Gamma\), \(\Pr(\mathrm{Go} \mid \gamma)\) and \(\Pr(\mathrm{NoGo} \mid \gamma)\) are obtained by fast indicator summations over the precomputed values without additional probability evaluations.

The optimal \(\gamma_{\mathrm{go}}\) is then selected as the smallest (or largest) value in \(\Gamma\) such that \(\Pr(\mathrm{Go} \mid \gamma_{\mathrm{go}})\) satisfies a user-specified criterion (e.g., \(\Pr(\mathrm{Go}) < 0.05\) under the null scenario), and \(\gamma_{\mathrm{nogo}}\) is selected analogously for the NoGo criterion.

6. Two-Endpoint Framework

6.1 Nine-Region Grid (Posterior Probability)

When two endpoints are evaluated jointly, the treatment effect vector is \(\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\theta_1, \theta_2)\). For each endpoint \(k = 1, 2\), three zones are defined by \(\theta_{\mathrm{TV}k}\) and \(\theta_{\mathrm{MAV}k}\):

  • Go zone: \(\theta_k > \theta_{\mathrm{TV}k}\)
  • Gray zone: \(\theta_{\mathrm{MAV}k} < \theta_k \le \theta_{\mathrm{TV}k}\)
  • NoGo zone: \(\theta_k \le \theta_{\mathrm{MAV}k}\)

Their Cartesian product defines nine mutually exclusive regions \(R_1, \ldots, R_9\):

Nine-region grid for two-endpoint posterior probability
Endpoint 1
θ1 > θTV1 θTV1 ≥ θ1 > θMAV1 θMAV1 ≥ θ1
Endpoint 2 θ2 > θTV2 R1 R4 R7
θTV2 ≥ θ2 > θMAV2 R2 R5 R8
θMAV2 ≥ θ2 R3 R6 R9

Region \(R_1\) (both endpoints exceed \(\theta_{\mathrm{TV}}\)) is typically designated as Go and \(R_9\) (both endpoints below \(\theta_{\mathrm{MAV}}\)) as NoGo.

6.2 Four-Region Grid (Predictive Probability)

For the predictive probability, each endpoint has a binary success/failure outcome, yielding four regions:

Four-region grid for two-endpoint predictive probability
Endpoint 1
θ1 > θNULL1 θ1 ≤ θNULL1
Endpoint 2 θ2 > θNULL2 R1 R3
θ2 ≤ θNULL2 R2 R4

6.3 Joint Posterior for Two Binary Endpoints

For two binary endpoints with response rates \((\pi_t^{(1)}, \pi_c^{(1)})\) (endpoint 1) and \((\pi_t^{(2)}, \pi_c^{(2)})\) (endpoint 2), a Dirichlet-multinomial model is used. In a treatment group of size \(n_t\), define the four mutually exclusive categories (both respond, only endpoint 1 responds, only endpoint 2 responds, neither responds) with probabilities \(\mathbf{p}_t = (p_{t,11}, p_{t,10}, p_{t,01}, p_{t,00})\).

A Dirichlet prior \(\mathrm{Dir}(\mathbf{a}_t)\) and the observed multinomial counts \(\mathbf{x}_t\) yield the conjugate posterior

\[\mathbf{p}_t \mid \mathbf{x}_t \;\sim\; \mathrm{Dir}(\mathbf{a}_t + \mathbf{x}_t).\]

The joint probabilities for the nine regions are computed by Monte Carlo draws from the Dirichlet posteriors in pbayespostpred2bin().

6.4 Joint Posterior for Two Continuous Endpoints

For two continuous endpoints, a Normal-Inverse-Wishart (N-Inv-W) conjugate prior is placed on \((\boldsymbol{\mu}_j, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j)\):

\[(\boldsymbol{\mu}_j, \boldsymbol{\Sigma}_j) \;\sim\; \mathrm{N\text{-}Inv\text{-}W}(\boldsymbol{\mu}_{0j},\, \kappa_{0j},\, \nu_{0j},\, \mathbf{V}_{0j}), \quad j = t, c.\]

Given \(n_j\) bivariate observations, the posterior parameters are updated analogously to the univariate case, yielding a matrix-\(t\) predictive distribution. The joint probability over rectangular regions is evaluated using the bivariate \(t\)-distribution via mvtnorm::pmvt() for the MC method, or a Moment-Matching approximation for the MM method (Theorem 3 of Homma and Yamaguchi, 2025).

7. Package Structure and Function Overview

Function Description
pbayespostpred1bin() Posterior/predictive probability, single binary endpoint
pbayespostpred1cont() Posterior/predictive probability, single continuous endpoint
pbayespostpred2bin() Region probabilities, two binary endpoints
pbayespostpred2cont() Region probabilities, two continuous endpoints
pbayesdecisionprob1bin() Go/NoGo/Gray probabilities, single binary endpoint
pbayesdecisionprob1cont() Go/NoGo/Gray probabilities, single continuous endpoint
pbayesdecisionprob2bin() Go/NoGo/Gray probabilities, two binary endpoints
pbayesdecisionprob2cont() Go/NoGo/Gray probabilities, two continuous endpoints
getgamma1bin() Optimal Go/NoGo thresholds, single binary endpoint
getgamma1cont() Optimal Go/NoGo thresholds, single continuous endpoint
getgamma2bin() Optimal Go/NoGo thresholds, two binary endpoints
getgamma2cont() Optimal Go/NoGo thresholds, two continuous endpoints
pbetadiff() CDF of difference of two Beta distributions
pbetabinomdiff() Beta-binomial posterior predictive probability
ptdiff_NI() CDF of difference of two t-distributions (numerical integration)
ptdiff_MC() CDF of difference of two t-distributions (Monte Carlo)
ptdiff_MM() CDF of difference of two t-distributions (Moment-Matching)
rdirichlet() Random sampler for the Dirichlet distribution
getjointbin() Joint binary probability from marginals and correlation
allmultinom() Enumerate all multinomial outcome combinations

8. Quick-Start Examples

8.1 Posterior Probability: Single Binary Endpoint 

# P(pi_treat - pi_ctrl > 0.05 | data)
# Observed: 7/10 responders (treatment), 3/10 (control)
pbayespostpred1bin(
  prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', theta0 = 0.05,
  n_t = 10, n_c = 10, y_t = 7, y_c = 3,
  a_t = 0.5, b_t = 0.5, a_c = 0.5, b_c = 0.5,
  m_t = NULL, m_c = NULL, z = NULL,
  ne_t = NULL, ne_c = NULL, ye_t = NULL, ye_c = NULL,
  alpha0e_t = NULL, alpha0e_c = NULL,
  lower.tail = FALSE
)
#> [1] 0.941619

8.2 Posterior Probability: Single Continuous Endpoint 

# P(mu_treat - mu_ctrl > 1.0 | data) using MM method
# Observed: mean 3.5 (treatment), 1.2 (control); SD ~ 2.0
pbayespostpred1cont(
  prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled', prior = 'vague',
  CalcMethod = 'MM', theta0 = 1.0, nMC = NULL,
  n_t = 10, n_c = 10,
  bar_y_t = 3.5, s_t = 2.0,
  bar_y_c = 1.2, s_c = 2.0,
  m_t = NULL, m_c = NULL,
  kappa0_t = NULL, kappa0_c = NULL, nu0_t = NULL, nu0_c = NULL,
  mu0_t = NULL, mu0_c = NULL, sigma0_t = NULL, sigma0_c = NULL,
  r = NULL,
  ne_t = NULL, ne_c = NULL, alpha0e_t = NULL, alpha0e_c = NULL,
  bar_ye_t = NULL, se_t = NULL, bar_ye_c = NULL, se_c = NULL
)
#> [1] 0.093935

8.3 Go/NoGo Decision: Operating Characteristics 

# Operating characteristics for single binary endpoint
oc_res <- pbayesdecisionprob1bin(
  prob      = 'posterior',
  design    = 'controlled',
  theta_TV  = 0.30,  theta_MAV = 0.10,  theta_NULL = NULL,
  gamma_go  = 0.80,  gamma_nogo = 0.20,
  pi_t      = seq(0.15, 0.9, l = 10),
  pi_c      = rep(0.15, 10),
  n_t = 10,  n_c = 10,
  a_t = 0.5, b_t = 0.5, a_c = 0.5, b_c = 0.5,
  z = NULL, m_t = NULL, m_c = NULL,
  ne_t = NULL, ne_c = NULL, ye_t = NULL, ye_c = NULL,
  alpha0e_t = NULL, alpha0e_c = NULL
)
print(oc_res)
#> Go/NoGo/Gray Decision Probabilities (Single Binary Endpoint) 
#> ---------------------------------------------------------------- 
#>   Probability type : posterior 
#>   Design           : controlled 
#>   Threshold(s)     : TV = 0.3, MAV = 0.1 
#>   Go  threshold    : gamma_go = 0.8 
#>   NoGo threshold   : gamma_nogo = 0.2 
#>   Sample size      : n_t = 10, n_c = 10 
#>   Prior (Beta)     : a_t = 0.5, a_c = 0.5, b_t = 0.5, b_c = 0.5 
#>   Miss handling    : error_if_Miss = TRUE, Gray_inc_Miss = FALSE 
#> ---------------------------------------------------------------- 
#>       pi_t pi_c     Go   Gray   NoGo
#>  0.1500000 0.15 0.0025 0.0532 0.9443
#>  0.2333333 0.15 0.0174 0.1494 0.8332
#>  0.3166667 0.15 0.0609 0.2647 0.6744
#>  0.4000000 0.15 0.1468 0.3569 0.4963
#>  0.4833333 0.15 0.2777 0.3940 0.3283
#>  0.5666667 0.15 0.4426 0.3658 0.1915
#>  0.6500000 0.15 0.6186 0.2860 0.0955
#>  0.7333333 0.15 0.7781 0.1835 0.0384
#>  0.8166667 0.15 0.8984 0.0904 0.0112
#>  0.9000000 0.15 0.9693 0.0289 0.0018
#> ----------------------------------------------------------------
plot(oc_res, base_size = 20)

8.4 Optimal Threshold Search 

# Find gamma_go  : smallest gamma s.t. Pr(Go)   < 0.05 under Null (pi_t = pi_c = 0.15)
# Find gamma_nogo: smallest gamma s.t. Pr(NoGo) < 0.20 under Alt  (pi_t = 0.35, pi_c = 0.15)
gg_res <- getgamma1bin(
  prob = 'posterior', design = 'controlled',
  theta_TV = 0.30, theta_MAV = 0.10, theta_NULL = NULL,
  pi_t_go = 0.15, pi_c_go = 0.15,
  pi_t_nogo = 0.35, pi_c_nogo = 0.15,
  target_go = 0.05, target_nogo = 0.20,
  n_t = 12L, n_c = 12L,
  a_t = 0.5, b_t = 0.5, a_c = 0.5, b_c = 0.5,
  z = NULL, m_t = NULL, m_c = NULL,
  ne_t = NULL, ne_c = NULL, ye_t = NULL, ye_c = NULL,
  alpha0e_t = NULL, alpha0e_c = NULL
)
plot(gg_res, base_size = 20)

9. Further Reading

  • vignette("single-continuous") — detailed examples for a single continuous endpoint, covering all three designs and calculation methods.
  • vignette("single-binary") — detailed examples for a single binary endpoint, covering all three designs and prior specifications.
  • vignette("two-continuous") — detailed examples for two continuous co-primary endpoints.
  • vignette("two-binary") — detailed examples for two binary co-primary endpoints.

On this page
